item.title: 0x05 lost while searching You
item.description: Deep within my heart, a longing stirs, yearning for a connection that remains elusive. It is a desire to be with you, a girl who has captured my thoughts and ignited a flame within my soul. Though our paths have yet to cross, the mere idea of you fills my days with hope and anticipation. In my mind, I paint pictures of your radiant smile, imagining the joy it would bring to my life.

item.title: 0x04 resisting temptation...
item.description: I’ve always believed that social media is useless. My main problem on most of these social media is the method of communication. The way that people communicate on these platforms is kind of more important than what they are talking about. Most of these platforms are exclusively for short-form content. It makes it nearly impossible to publish anything meaningful on them. I can’t stand it when people pretend to be a victim of their own behavior, although they aren’t the ones in control.

item.title: 0x03 a love letter to forums
item.description: Time goes on, technology is innovated upon, however when I was younger, I used to be enthralled through forums. I don’t understand the particulars of it, but there’s something oddly satisfying about creating a post on a board, and having to take a seat and refresh the page numerous times until you get a response. That’s something these days’ technology has taken from us. Why would people need to sit on a forum, create a submission asking how to fix a problem or spark some kind of conversation while they could go on Discord and get a response damn close to instantly?

item.title: 0x02 systemdo or systemdon't
item.description: Often times in the Linux community, people will tell you things like “systemd is terrible” and that you shouldn’t use it. Then they will go on a triad of reasons why systemd is awful. Now, to any normal or knowledgeable user, some of these arguments in opposition to systemd can be considered valid. But to any other person, specially users new to Linux, these are horribly deceptive reasons to use something apart from systemd.

item.title: 0x01 __always_inline, linux, and musl
item.description: If you’re trying to install kiss linux (or any non-glibc distribution) with a linux 5.x kernel, you may find that you’ll run into an error trying to build the kernel. /usr/include/linux/byteorder/little_endian.h:44:8: error: unknown type name '__always_inline' On a system running glibc, this isn’t actually an issue; __always_inline is defined. This isn’t the case on systems that are using musl or other libc’s though. So, as a quick fix, open up /usr/include/linux/swab.

pure of heart, dumb of ass # So this is my “blog” of sorts. A little about me, about the work I do, my system, etc. This isn’t strictly tech related. This is just a blog for me to write whatever comes to mind and a place for me to write about interesting topics and explain concepts to other people. If you find this website ugly - It’s minimal.
How to reach me? # e-mail: # personal — info.senath@gmail.com other — dragnosmz@proton.me I’m on, # Github , Steam and Patreon
